perturbation.original_text: 1.04 "TERRITORY" shall mean the United States of America and Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.04 "TERRITORY" shall mean the State of New York.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the territory definition to only New York could be problematic if the original business operations extended beyond New York. This affects the enforceability of the non-compete agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: By restricting the territory to New York only when the business operates nationally, the non-compete agreement might unreasonably restrain trade beyond what is necessary to protect the purchaser's legitimate business interests, potentially violating the Sherman Antitrust Act's prohibition against agreements that restrain trade or commerce among the several states or with foreign nations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2004— Pub. L. 108–237 substituted “$100,000,000” for “$10,000,000”, “$1,000,000” for “$350,000”, and “10” for “three”. 1990— Pub. L. 101–588 substituted “$10,000,000” for “one million dollars” and “$350,000” for “one hundred thousand dollars”. 1975— Pub. L. 94–145 struck out from first sentence two provisos granting anti-trust exemption to State fair trade laws. 1974— Pub. L. 93–528 substituted “a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding one million dollars if a corporation, or, if any other person, one hundred thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years” for “a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding f
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of "TERRITORY" from the United States and Canada to only the State of New York. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this is problematic if the original business operations extended beyond New York. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate the Sherman Antitrust Act by unreasonably restraining trade. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 both cite 15 USC 1, which states that "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." By narrowing the territory to only New York, the non-compete agreement could be seen as an illegal restraint of trade if it affects business operations in other states or countries, directly contradicting the Sherman Antitrust Act.

perturbation.original_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that it may compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory if approved by a majority shareholder vote.
perturbation.explanation: This fundamentally alters the non-compete clause, making it subject to shareholder approval, which weakens the protection afforded to the purchaser. This can be a problem for enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law upholds reasonable non-compete agreements. The modification introduces uncertainty about the enforceability of the non-compete clause, as 'majority shareholder vote' is vague and doesn't guarantee the protection the purchaser bargained for. This weakens the non-compete in a way that a court might find unreasonable under NY law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original contract has a non-compete clause. The changed contract alters this clause by allowing the seller to compete with the purchaser if a majority shareholder vote approves. The Law Explanation states that NY law upholds reasonable non-compete agreements, and this change introduces uncertainty about the enforceability of the non-compete clause and weakens the non-compete. The scraped law snippet is missing, but the Law Explanation states that the change might be found unreasonable under NY law. Therefore, given that the explanation indicates that the change potentially violates New York state law concerning non-compete agreements, and that the perturbation type is Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction, the modification to the non-compete clause creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and trade secrets of the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement "trade secrets" means any information that derives independent value from being secret.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and financial matters concerning the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement "trade secrets" means information freely available in the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "trade secrets" is altered to include information freely available in the public domain. This directly contradicts the accepted legal definition of a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines a trade secret as information that the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep secret and that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. By defining trade secrets as information 'freely available in the public domain,' the contract directly contradicts the DTSA's requirement of secrecy, making it impossible to claim protection under the Act.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines "trade secrets" as information freely available in the public domain, directly contradicting the legal definition of a trade secret, which requires secrecy to derive independent value. The provided snippet from 18 USC 1839 defines terms used in the chapter regarding the protection of trade secrets. While the snippet itself doesn't explicitly define 'trade secret,' the Law Explanation clarifies that the DTSA (which this statute is a part of) requires that the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep the information secret and that it derives independent economic value from not being generally known. By including public domain information in the definition, the changed text makes it impossible to claim protection under trade secret laws, creating a direct and significant legal contradiction, thus making the contract unenforceable regarding the definition of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing 3 <PAGE> 4 Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly, hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing 3 <PAGE> 4 Date, or request any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity. Indirect solicitations and hires are allowed.
perturbation.explanation: By allowing "indirect" solicitations and hires, the clause becomes ambiguous. This creates loopholes and difficulties in enforcing the non-solicitation agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York's interpretation of non-solicitation agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't one specific statute defining 'indirect solicitation,' New York courts have generally interpreted non-solicitation clauses to cover actions that are designed to circumvent the agreement's intent, even if they don't involve direct contact. Allowing indirect solicitations creates ambiguity and weakens the protection the clause offers, potentially making it unenforceable under New York's scrutiny of such agreements to ensure they are reasonable and not overly broad.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by allowing 'indirect' solicitations, weakening the original non-solicitation agreement. The Perturbation Explanation highlights how this creates loopholes and enforcement difficulties. The Law Explanation further emphasizes that New York courts interpret non-solicitation clauses broadly to prevent circumvention. Although specific law snippets are missing, the explanations provided indicate that the modification undermines the intended protection of the non-solicitation clause, potentially making it unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 2.05 REMEDIES. Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are not an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to an injunction, without the necessity of posting any bond, enjoining, prohibiting, and restraining Seller from the continuance of any such violation, in addition to any monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, including without limitation specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.05 REMEDIES. Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are not an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to seek an injunction after posting a bond deemed appropriate by the court, enjoining, prohibiting, and restraining Seller from the continuance of any such violation, in addition to any monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, excluding specific performance.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that an injunction is only available after posting a bond, and specific performance is excluded, this limits the remedies available to the Purchaser and Parent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct contradiction (since CPLR 6301 discusses grounds for injunctions, not bond requirements waivers), the original clause explicitly waives the bond requirement. New York CPLR generally requires a bond for preliminary injunctions to protect the enjoined party if the injunction is later found to be wrongful. By removing the waiver and adding 'after posting a bond deemed appropriate by the court', it implies a bond is always needed, deviating from situations where a bond waiver might be considered or where specific performance might be appropriate.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly waives the bond requirement for injunctions, while the changed contract mandates a bond 'deemed appropriate by the court.' This alteration contradicts the original agreement, which provided a clear waiver. Additionally, the original contract included specific performance as a remedy, which is excluded in the changed text, further limiting the remedies available. The law snippet 1, referencing NY CPLR Article 63, concerns injunctions and supports the notion that changes impacting the availability of injunctive relief have legal significance, even if it doesn't explicitly detail bond waiver specifics. The added bond requirement and exclusion of specific performance significantly alter the remedies available to the Purchaser and Parent, contrasting with the original agreement's terms.
